mnemonics.title: A.M.I. - The Software Blueprint
mnemonics.content: Abstraction Means Ignoring details to focus on what matters. Think of a car dashboard - you don't need to understand the engine to drive!

mnemonics.title: Modular Magic
mnemonics.content: Modularity Means Organize code into separate, reusable chunks. Like LEGO blocks - each piece works alone but combines to build bigger things!

mnemonics.title: Independent Investigators
mnemonics.content: Independence Means each module works on its own with minimal connections. Like different departments in a company - HR doesn't need to know IT's code!

acronyms.usage: Remember the three pillars of software design when writing code

acronyms.expansion: Single Responsibility, Open-Closed, Liskov Substitution, Interface Segregation, Dependency Inversion
acronyms.usage: Use these 5 principles to achieve modularity and independence in object-oriented design
Abstract way for modules to communicate without knowing internal workings

visualTips.visualization: Picture a TV remote - you see buttons (interface) but don't see the circuits inside (implementation). The remote abstracts away complexity!
Imagine a phone charger - it's a separate module that works with different phones. If it breaks, you replace just that piece, not the whole phone!
Think of wall sockets in your house - each works independently. If one stops working, others still function. Modules should be as independent as wall sockets!

analogies.analogy: Driving a car - you use steering, accelerator, brake (abstract interface) without needing to understand combustion engines or gear systems (hidden details).
A modular sofa - individual sections can be rearranged, removed, or replaced independently. Software modules work the same way!
Department stores - each department (electronics, clothing, food) operates independently but together forms one building. Changes in one don't break others.
